content.description:
Event handlers in Hobson views capture data at render time, but the database may have been updated since then (especially via [silent updates](item://c0c0c0c0-0010-0000-0000-000000000000)). Using stale closure data for database writes causes bugs where changes silently revert.

## The Problem

JavaScript closures capture variables from their enclosing scope. When a view renders, handlers close over the current item state:

```javascript
export async function render(item, api) {
  const attachments = item.attachments;  // Snapshot at render time

  titlebar.onmousedown = () => {
    // BUG: Uses stale data! Other windows may have moved since render.
    const maxZ = Math.max(...attachments.map(c => c.z));
  };
}
```

After silent updates (drag, z-index changes), the database holds current state but the closure still has the render-time snapshot. This causes:

- **Windows jumping to wrong positions** — stale position data overwrites current positions
- **Z-index calculations using old data** — maxZ computed from stale values
- **Position updates reverting other windows** — writing stale attachment array back

## The Solution: Always Read Fresh

In any event handler that reads or writes item data, fetch the current state from the database:

```javascript
titlebar.onmousedown = async () => {
  const freshItem = await api.get(item.id);  // Current state from DB
  const freshChildren = freshItem.attachments;
  const maxZ = Math.max(...freshChildren.map(c => c.z));
};
```

## Critical Locations

Any function that:
1. **Reads** item data and makes decisions based on it
2. **Writes** item data back to the database
3. Runs **asynchronously** after render time (event handlers, callbacks, timers)

Must fetch fresh state first. Key examples:

- `updateChild()` — MUST read fresh attachments before modifying
- Click-to-front handler — MUST read fresh z-indices for maxZ
- Drag end handler — MUST read fresh state before saving final position
- Any handler that modifies the parent item's attachments array

## Red Flags

Watch for these patterns in code review:

- Variables captured from outer scope used inside `async` handlers
- Array/object snapshots used for database writes
- Calculations based on "current" data that might be stale
- `item.attachments.map(...)` inside an event handler (should be `freshItem.attachments.map(...)`)

## The Rule

> **If you're in an event handler and need item data, call `api.get()` first.**

The cost is one IndexedDB read (fast, local). The benefit is correctness.

## See Also

- [Silent Update Pattern](item://c0c0c0c0-0010-0000-0000-000000000000) — Why closures become stale
- [Spatial Windowing](item://c0c0c0c0-0020-0000-0000-000000000000) — Where this pattern is most critical
